name: Continuous Drive-Dynamics with Urgency-Threshold Policy
description: ODE-based continuous-time drive dynamics with a deterministic threshold-and-gradient policy. The agent maintains a scalar energy level governed by linear decay and discrete intake events. Drive is computed as the asymmetric (deficit-only) squared deviation from setpoint. A cybernetic negative-feedback controller uses two thresholds (D_crit and D_low) and the drive velocity (rate of change) to gate between three behavioural modes: REST, EAT, and FORAGE. In FORAGE mode, the agent follows a greedy Manhattan-distance gradient toward the nearest food resource. No learning occurs — the agent is purely reactive. Grounded in Cannon (1929) cybernetic homeostasis and Hull (1943) drive-reduction theory.

variables.name: energy_level
variables.description: Scalar physiological variable representing normalised energy/nutrient store

variables.name: setpoint
variables.description: Ideal target value for the energy level

variables.name: drive
variables.description: Motivational urgency signal: squared deficit from setpoint. Zero when energy >= setpoint; increases quadratically as energy drops below setpoint.

variables.name: previous_drive
variables.description: Drive value from the previous time step, used to compute drive velocity

variables.name: drive_velocity
variables.description: Finite-difference approximation of drive rate of change. Positive means drive is worsening; negative means drive is improving.

variables.name: behavioural_mode
variables.description: Current action mode selected by threshold gating: REST, EAT, or FORAGE

parameters.name: setpoint
parameters.source: Matched to Keramati & Gutkin (2014) normalised energy target

parameters.name: eating_restoration
parameters.source: Calibrated; food value per consumption event

parameters.name: movement_cost
parameters.source: Calibrated; action metabolic cost for movement

parameters.name: low_drive_threshold
parameters.source: Calibrated; below this the agent rests to conserve energy

rules.description: Energy dynamics: discrete-time ODE approximation. Energy decays by lambda_decay each step, is reduced by c_move if agent moved, and increased by c_eat if agent ate. Clipped to [0,1].
rules.pseudocode:
moved = 1 if action in ['move_up','move_down','move_left','move_right'] else 0
ate = 1 if (action == 'eat' and last_action_result.get('consumed', False)) else 0
h = clip(h + (-lambda_decay + c_eat * ate - c_move * moved), 0.0, 1.0)

rules.description: Asymmetric drive function: only deficit (h < h*) generates drive. When h >= h*, drive is zero. This follows Hull (1943) where deprivation, not excess, is motivating.
Drive velocity via finite difference: approximates dD/dt to detect whether the organism's condition is improving or deteriorating.
Nearest-resource gradient: for each movement action, compute whether it brings the agent closer to the nearest food resource (Manhattan distance reduction).
rules.pseudocode:
# For action a, compute the position the agent would occupy
new_x, new_y = position_after(current_x, current_y, a)
d_current = manhattan_distance_to_nearest_food(current_x, current_y, food_list)
d_after = manhattan_distance_to_nearest_food(new_x, new_y, food_list)
g_a = d_current - d_after  # positive means a brings agent closer

rules.description: Mode selection: urgency-gated switching between REST, EAT, and FORAGE based on drive level, drive velocity, and food availability.

decision_logic.description: Deterministic cybernetic controller: compute drive and drive velocity, gate into a behavioural mode using thresholds, then execute the appropriate action for that mode. No learning — purely reactive negative feedback.
pseudocode: food_here = any(f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'] for f in food_list)
d_current = min(manhattan(current_x, current_y, f['x'], f['y']) for f in food_list)
d_after = min(manhattan(nx, ny, f['x'], f['y']) for f in food_list)
action = best_action if best_action else random_choice(move_actions)

perception_to_variables.food_at_current_cell: any(f['x'] == perception['x'] and f['y'] == perception['y'] for f in perception['resources'].get('food', []))

env_mapping.reward_source: No external reward is used. The agent is purely reactive, driven by internal drive dynamics. The env ConsumeEffect reward (1.0) is ignored.

expected_behaviors.description: Immediate foraging when depleted: agent transitions to FORAGE mode as soon as drive exceeds D_crit or is increasing, and moves directly toward nearest food
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h=0.4 (well below setpoint) → assert mode == 'FORAGE' and agent moves toward nearest food

expected_behaviors.description: Rest when sated: agent stays still when energy is at or above setpoint (drive near zero), conserving energy
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h=0.85 → compute D=(0.8-0.85) clamped to 0 → D=0 < D_low → assert action == 'stay'

expected_behaviors.description: Eat immediately when on food and hungry: if food is present at the agent's cell and drive > 0, agent eats before attempting movement
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place agent on food cell with h=0.5 → assert action == 'eat'

expected_behaviors.description: Energy oscillates around setpoint: over many steps with food available, energy oscillates in a band near h* as the agent alternates between foraging/eating and resting
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: run 500 steps with food regenerating → compute mean h → assert 0.6 < mean_h < 0.9

expected_behaviors.description: Drive velocity detects worsening: even when drive is below D_crit, if drive is increasing (dD_dt > 0) the agent preemptively switches to FORAGE mode
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: set h=0.68 (D=0.0144 < D_crit=0.15) but D_prev=0.01 so dD_dt=0.0044>0 → assert mode == 'FORAGE'

references: Cannon, W.B. (1929). Organization for physiological homeostasis. Foundational cybernetic negative-feedback framework.
Hull, C.L. (1943). Principles of Behavior. Drive-reduction theory: deprivation generates motivational drive directing behaviour.

Keramati, M. & Gutkin, B. (2014). Homeostatic reinforcement learning for integrating reward collection and physiological stability. eLife 3:e04811. Referenced for quadratic drive function and setpoint calibration.
